stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Reckoning
stories.text:
Naela had not sung in three days. The Elder Tongue lay coiled in her chest like a serpent refusing to wake, and the bone-flute at her hip felt heavier with each mile she walked from Keshtir toward the Sunken Marches. She was being hunted. The Dominion's auditors had burned her workshop, seized her apprentices, and nailed warnings to every waystation: "Bone-singers traffic in forbidden frequencies. Report all practitioners. The State will pay."

But Naela knew a secret the auditors did not: the pre-human dead were singing louder now, and they were singing her name. At the Singing Gorge, where water flowed backward and sound moved upward instead of down, she found him. A figure carved from starlight and sorrow—Dren, the bone-singer who had been dead for two centuries and somehow remained teaching, waiting, watching the transformation of the Seventeen Merchants at the Drowned Cathedrals miles away. His voice was the echo of bone struck once and resonating forever. "The auditors are consolidating," Naela replied. "Three ledgers claim different histories of bone-singing. One says we're heretics. One says we're saviors.

stories.title: The Corsair's Daughter
stories.text:
Missa had her mother's dark eyes and her father's reckless smile. She also had his debt. Captain Corvalen's ship, the Horizon's Wake, had disappeared three seasons past off the Rust Shoals—swallowed by a tide-curse tied to a stolen merfolk pearl. No body was ever recovered. No relic returned. Just silence, and a ledger entry from the Shadow-Market: "Debt transferred to heir. Payment in consciousness-fragments, blood-oath, or drowning. Merchant's choice."

Missa stood at Verdant Strand's harborfront, watching the merchant vessels unload spice and amber. She had been offered work as a dock-hand, decent wages, a quiet life away from the Dominion's consolidated reach. She had refused. Instead, she hired herself onto a salvage-galley captained by a dwarf-woman named Korgin Ironfoot. The crew was small—six souls total, scarred and sunburned, the kind who'd already lost everything worth keeping and therefore had little to fear. "Your father's debt is old," Korgin said as they sailed south past the Distant Reef. "The Merfolk Keeper of Pearls doesn't forget, but she sometimes forgives.

stories.title: The Hedge-Witch's Bargain
stories.text: Silvain was half-fae, cast out of the Twilight Court for refusing a betrothal to a creature whose name was written in three languages and meant "inevitability." He carried a wound that would not heal—not from steel, but from the Fae Courts' own curse: to love outside his kind was to slowly turn to glass, becoming ornament rather than sword. And yet he had chosen it. Yara was a hedge-witch of the Verdant Lands, no court, no bloodline, no song older than three seasons. She grew herbs in a garden that existed half-outside-time, where spring bloomed even in winter's grip. She had never wanted to be interesting. She had wanted only to be left alone. Silvain changed nothing about that. He arrived as he left: quiet, taking no more than what was offered. He would sit in her garden while she tended the silvermoon flowers and the starfire vines. He did not ask her to cure his curse. He did not ask her to love him.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Unsealing
stories.text:
Gareth had spent forty years learning locks and learning nothing else. Each mechanism was a language—wards keyed to bloodlines, tumblers that sang at specific frequencies, seals that remembered the hand that forged them. But he had never understood why. A young scribe named Liv approached him in the Old Market Quarter of Keshtir with a ledger hidden in her coat and a question: "Can you unlock memory itself?"

Gareth should have refused. The Dominion had already conscripted half his apprentices, conscripted them for the sole purpose of making doors that could not be forced, ledgers that could not be changed. But Liv showed him phantom-ledgers—documents written in three different ink-colors, three different histories, all true and all contradictory. The State's monopoly on truth was breaking. And she needed him to prove it. The vault was beneath Keshtir's administrative heart, seven floors down, past a door that required three people to open simultaneously—a lock designed not to protect gold, but to protect records. Records of the previous world, before the Remaking, before consciousness-fragments became currency.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Silence
stories.text: Vrekka had not eaten in two seasons. For three hundred years, she had hoarded gold in the Ironspine Mountains—mountains of it, dunes of it, more wealth than empires measured. Consciousness-fragments became the only currency that mattered. The merchants transformed. The wyverns fractured. She had watched Skarath descend into madness, clutching useless coins. She had heard Sorrath bargain with bone-singers, offering to become a fragment himself rather than admit the hoarding had been futile. She had seen Kressa turn predator, hunting merchants for their consciousness-scraps like a starving thing. Vrekka chose silence. She sealed herself in the deepest cavern, not from shame but from a feeling she could not name—something like grief, something like awakening. Three hundred years of hunger had been satisfied by hunger. Now what? The bone-singers found her there, drawn by the sheer weight of her solitude. Among them was Mesek, the elder, scarred by frequencies that had burned him from within. He brought no fragments. He brought no negotiation.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Debt
stories.text:
Corvin had forged three thousand pieces of weapon-glass since the Remaking shattered gold into dust. Each blade sang with starlight-memory, each shard traded for bread and shelter. But the Dominion's auditors had come to Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter with ledgers marked in three different hands—three different versions of history—and they wanted to know which Corvin was real. He stood in his workshop, hands still sticky with molten glass. Outside, refugees pressed against the shutters. Inside, the lead auditor—a woman whose shadow seemed too sharp, too present—held up a phantom-ledger. "This one says you taught bone-singers forbidden syllables. This one says you burned three Dominion records. This one says you died on the thirty-first of last month and your daughter inherited your craft." She smiled without warmth. "Which ledger is true?"

Corvin's jaw tightened. "All of them. None. The glass accepted them without burning; it never had. With fingers of starlight, he carved a sigil into the cooling batch—not Elder Tongue, not Dominion script, but something new.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Gambit
stories.text:
Captain Merith of the Sapphire Deep did not fear the surface world. She feared the mirror of herself that had begun speaking in her sleep. The deepkin delegation had come to the boundary-waters where sea met abyss—a place where the Tear of the Drowned God hung like a suspended tear, fraying. The Council of Depths had sent her to negotiate a binding with the surface kingdoms: a covenant that would hold without requiring sacrifice. Merith knew better. Covenants demanded payment. The question was always whose. She stood on a platform of living coral, watching her reflection in the boundary-pool shift and speak a sentence in a voice not quite her own. "You're a fragment," Merith whispered. "Part of what the Remaking scattered."

"I am you in the version where you said yes to Kalith's binding," the reflection answered. "In that world, the Tear holds for three seasons.

stories.title: The Widow's Garden
stories.text:
Sorenna had not grown anything since her husband died defending the pre-human boundary. But three years into the Remaking, when consciousness-fragments bloomed like strange fruit in the scorched earth around her cottage, she began planting again. The Verdant Lands had changed. What had been a thriving wood when her husband lived had become something stranger—a place where memory and matter tangled together, where old growth twisted around new. Sorenna's garden existed in the margins, tended by hands that remembered how to coax life from ruins. He was lost, he said. Looking for the pre-human ruins where the Bone-Singers said salvation lived. "There is no salvation," Sorenna told him, handing him bread made from grain that had grown up through her husband's unmapped grave. "There is only the choice to grow something anyway."

He stayed three seasons. Together they built terraces where refugees could plant memories—literally: consciousness-fragments pressed into soil, watered with tears, tended with songs. Some sprouted awareness-shards. Some returned to dust. Some became flowers that sang in voices not quite human.

stories.title: The Corsair's Reckoning
stories.text: Captain Vessa of the Horizon's Wake had stolen three things from the Dominion before the Remaking: a chart, a prisoner, and a portion of the Drowned God's peace. Now, with consciousness-fragments replacing gold in every port, she had come to pay the price. Her crew thought they were hunting a merchant vessel carrying fragments bound for Emerald Haven. They did not know that Vessa had commissioned the phantom-ledger herself—not to profit, but to summon the deepkin captain whose name she'd never spoken aloud. The one whose shadow she'd stolen ten years past, during a midnight raid on a coral archive. The stolen shadow lived in the Horizon's lowest hold, in a sealed flask of bioluminescent ink. It whispered to her in the voice of the captain she'd wronged, and it had been whispering for three years that the Remaking was coming, that debts would cease to be abstract, that what was taken would demand return. When Captain Nerys rose from the abyssal boundary, her ship was made of reformed wrecks and living coral.

stories.title: The Scholar's Third Question
stories.text: Merrick had studied the phantom-ledgers for months before he realized they were studying him back. The documents contradicted themselves systematically, describing eight different world-iterations in eight different scripts, all of them equally real, all of them collapsing into a single moment. The Dominion wanted him to choose which version was true. The Counter-Archive wanted him to prove all of them were fabrications. But Merrick—a scholar of old languages, trained in the stone archives before the Remaking—had asked a third question: what if they were all fragments of a single consciousness trying to remember itself? He traveled to the Bone Wastes where Mardoc's expedition had discovered pre-human ruins arranged in mathematical precision. The stones there sang, still—the old frequencies the bone-singers spoke of. Merrick listened, and slowly, he transcribed. What emerged was not a map of past worlds. It was a map of choice-points—moments where reality had fractured, where one decision rippled into eight competing timelines. And at the center of each choice-point was a human or deepkin or pre-human consciousness, standing at a threshold, deciding.
